region_of_origin: Ireland (Diageo); Singapore market variant at 6.8% ABV
food_subgroup: Alcoholic beverages — dark stout beer, 6.8% ABV

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI specific to Guinness Foreign Extra Stout. FOODON_03301435 (beer, food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A037G is the closest FoodEx2 category for stout/porter beer; no code specific to Guinness FES Singapore variant exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 53410008 (Beer, substance) is the closest available code; no stout-specific SNOMED CT code identified.

serving.description: 1 can (250 ml) — standard Singapore retail; 1 pint (500 ml) — common Singapore bar serving
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 250 ml can of Guinness Foreign Extra Stout as sold in Singapore retail, equivalent to approximately 150 kcal and 17 g of alcohol at 6.8% ABV.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Beer, stout (NDB 14007) + Diageo Singapore product label (Guinness FES 6.8% ABV)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and Diageo Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros and micronutrients adapted from USDA SR Legacy stout reference (NDB 14007) and cross-referenced with Diageo Singapore product label for the 6.8% ABV Singapore-market Guinness FES. Energy value of 60 kcal/100 ml is consistent with the higher ABV formulation (alcohol contributes ~7 kcal/g; 6.8% ABV = ~5.4 g alcohol/100 ml = ~37.8 kcal from alcohol alone, plus ~5.7 g carbohydrate = ~22.8 kcal, total ~60 kcal/100 ml). Micronutrient values (iron, folate, B vitamins, minerals) sourced from USDA SR Legacy stout reference as the closest approved analogue.

gi_source.title: Alcoholic beverages and glycemic index — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (beer, regular); Buyken et al. 2014 peer-reviewed reference for beer GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Guinness FES 6.8% ABV Singapore variant. GI of 66 (medium) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for regular beer (GI ~66, glucose reference). Guinness FES has higher carbohydrate content (5.7 g/100 ml vs ~3.5 g/100 ml for lager) but the higher alcohol content (6.8% ABV) suppresses postprandial glucose response via hepatic glucose inhibition, making the effective GI comparable to standard beer. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving = (66 × 14.25 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.4 GL units. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of Guinness FES has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026.

hypertension.key_factor: High alcohol content — 6.8% ABV delivers 17 g alcohol per 250 ml can and 27 g per 500 ml pint, exceeding the daily recommended alcohol limit in a single bar serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension must be explicitly counselled that Singapore Guinness FES at 6.8% ABV is 36% stronger per volume than Irish draught Guinness (4.2% ABV); a single 500 ml pint exceeds the daily alcohol limit and carries significant pressor risk — limit to one 250 ml can maximum and preferably abstain.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sulfonylurea hypoglycaemia masking risk amplified by higher ABV (6.8%); unpredictable net glycaemic effect from combined alcohol-mediated hepatic glucose suppression and 5.7 g/100 ml carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes on sulfonylureas must be specifically counselled that Guinness FES at 6.8% ABV carries a proportionally greater hypoglycaemia-masking risk than standard lager; a patient instructed to limit to two Tiger Beers must be told that one 500 ml pint of Guinness FES exceeds that alcohol equivalent — consume only with food and with explicit hypoglycaemia risk counselling, or abstain.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol dose per serving is high — 27 g per 500 ml pint (2.7 standard drinks); chronic excess alcohol intake is an independent risk factor for cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, and hypertriglyceridaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with established cardiovascular disease should be advised that a single 500 ml pint of Guinness FES delivers 2.7 standard drinks of alcohol, exceeding safe daily limits; the modest iron content (1.5 mg per pint) does not confer cardiovascular benefit and should not be used to justify consumption — limit to one 250 ml can on occasion or abstain.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Hepatotoxic interaction with methotrexate; higher ABV increases alcohol dose per serving proportionally
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate must abstain from all alcohol including Guinness FES; the traditional belief in Singapore that Guinness has health benefits due to iron content does not alter the hepatotoxicity risk of the methotrexate-alcohol interaction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Guinness Foreign Extra Stout (FES) as sold in Singapore is a dark stout beer brewed by Diageo at 6.8% ABV — substantially and clinically significantly stronger than the well-known Irish draught Guinness at 4.2% ABV. This distinction is the single most important clinical fact about this product. A 500 ml pint of Singapore Guinness FES contains approximately 27 g of alcohol (2.7 standard drinks), while a 500 ml pint of Irish draught Guinness contains approximately 16.7 g of alcohol (1.67 standard drinks). A patient or clinician who uses the widely cited 4.2% ABV figure for Singapore Guinness FES will underestimate alcohol intake by approximately 60%. Guinness FES is brewed from roasted barley malt, water, hops, and yeast. The roasting process gives it its characteristic near-black colour, bitter-sweet flavour, and coffee-like aroma. Per 100 ml, it provides approximately 60 kcal, 0.5 g protein, 0.0 g fat (fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — beer contains no fat), and 5.7 g carbohydrate. The modest iron content (~0.3 mg/100 ml, or 1.5 mg per 500 ml pint) is frequently cited in Singapore as a health justification for drinking Guinness — this is a dietary myth. The 1.5 mg iron per pint is nutritionally minor relative to the recommended daily intake of 8–18 mg and does not offset the alcohol-related health risks. Guinness FES is commonly consumed in Singapore pubs, bars, restaurants, and hawker centres. It is sold in 250 ml cans at retail and served in 500 ml pints at bars. Clinicians counselling patients in Singapore must be aware that 'Guinness' in the local context almost always refers to the 6.8% FES variant, not the 4.2% Irish draught.
common_user_queries: is Guinness good for health Singapore
how many standard drinks in a pint of Guinness Singapore

confidence_reason: Core macros and energy value are consistent with USDA SR Legacy stout reference (NDB 14007) and cross-validated against the Diageo Singapore product label for the 6.8% ABV formulation. The 6.8% ABV is confirmed by the Singapore market product. Micronutrient values are sourced from the closest approved analogue (USDA SR Legacy stout). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for regular beer.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — beer is a fermented grain beverage and contains no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy stout reference. sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy stout reference — residual fermentable sugars are consumed during fermentation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy stout reference. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg are confirmed zero or below detection threshold per USDA SR Legacy stout reference. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for stout beer is not reported in USDA SR Legacy stout reference as these values are not nutritionally meaningful for a fat-free beverage. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific 6.8% ABV Singapore Guinness FES variant is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central or Diageo Singapore label data as of April 2026; estimated water content is approximately 88–90 g/100 ml by difference but this is not confirmed by an approved source and is therefore set to -1. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: Singapore Guinness FES is 6.8% ABV, NOT 4.2% ABV. A 500 ml pint contains 27 g alcohol (2.7 standard drinks). Clinicians must not use the Irish draught Guinness 4.2% ABV figure for Singapore patients. The traditional belief that Guinness has significant iron health benefits is a dietary myth — 1.5 mg iron per 500 ml pint is nutritionally minor. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (regular beer); no direct GI measurement for Guinness FES 6.8% ABV has been published.
